hypothesis_text: Safety wage-setting is more likely than non-safety wage-setting to combine occupational pressure with formal or quasi-formal conversion channels.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: Binary codify cannot measure intensity; non-safety also has conversion channels. 2026-07-14: Arlington MA public_works (non-safety) shows the same hazard-adjacent bundle shape as its matched fire counterpart is missing (no minimum-staffing/hazard language on the non-safety side) but DOES share interest-arbitration/impasse-backstop and no-strike coverage with fire -- a partial counterexample worth tracking alongside Houston's non-safety impasse evidence.
next_sources_needed: New matched triads in PA/NJ/IL/NY or other public-CBA states
decision_rule_for_strengthening: Multiple new states show safety pressure plus conversion bundles compared with matched non-safety channels.
decision_rule_for_weakening: New states show similar pressure/conversion bundles in matched non-safety units or safety rows lack these channels.
notes: Keep bounded to evidence patterns. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Philadelphia (PA) and Newark/Trenton (NJ) are the leading uningested candidates to test this hypothesis outside Ohio. See pa_nj_state_city_claim_notes_2026-07-12.md. | 2026-07-14: see H2 for the fuller Arlington finding. | 2026-07-14: see H2's notes for the fuller Philadelphia/Trenton finding -- the pattern across non-Ohio matched designs tested so far (Houston, Arlington, Philadelphia, Trenton) is genuinely mixed, not a clean confirmation or disconfirmation of H1's core prediction.

hypothesis_text: Police/fire wage-setting more often has formal impasse, arbitration, factfinding, or statutory bargaining backstops than matched non-safety units.
current_best_evidence: Ohio safety impasse evidence and San Antonio within-document arbitration distinction. Philadelphia police's Act 111 award (2026-07-14) is a clean, affirmative fourth example of a safety-side formal impasse backstop.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: Houston non-safety has impasse/arbitration evidence; grievance arbitration is widespread. 2026-07-14: Arlington MA public_works (non-safety) also shows genuine interest_arbitration_or_formal_impasse_backstop evidence, in a real exact-cycle matched pair with Arlington fire -- both cite the SAME town-wide MA statutory impasse mechanism (mediation and fact-finding under Ch. 1078 of the Acts of 1973), suggesting a state/institutional-level symmetry (at least in this one MA town) rather than an occupation-specific safety backstop. This is the second matched non-safety unit (after Houston TX) found to share its safety counterpart's formal impasse channel.
needed_state_types: states with public safety arbitration or formal impasse systems
decision_rule_for_strengthening: Safety backstops recur across multiple new states while matched non-safety rows do not show analogous new-term impasse channels.
decision_rule_for_weakening: Matched non-safety rows regularly show comparable formal wage-setting backstops.
notes: Maintain interest vs grievance distinction. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Philadelphia's city-hosted fire interest-arbitration award and Newark/Trenton/Paterson's PERC decision histories are candidate evidence, pending ingestion. | 2026-07-14: 2-for-2 non-Ohio matched pairs now show non-safety impasse-backstop evidence (Houston TX, Arlington MA) -- not yet 'regular' enough per this hypothesis's own weakening rule to downgrade support_level, but a pattern worth actively tracking in the next codify wave (Philadelphia/Trenton would be a third, cross-framework test). | 2026-07-14 update: Philadelphia PA now provides a THIRD data point after Ohio and Houston/Arlington -- and it goes the OTHER way. Philadelphia police/fire show genuine, affirmative interest-arbitration content (Act 111); Philadelphia's codified non-safety row (DC47 term sheet) shows rich grievance-arbitration content but NO interest-arbitration finding at all -- matching Ohio's asymmetric pattern, not Arlington's/Houston's symmetric one. Trenton NJ is a fourth, structurally different case: both police and fire CBA text explicitly EXCLUDES wages from the CBA's own internal arbitration article (deferring to NJ's external interest-arbitration statute, not visible in this window), while Trenton's non-safety row shows no interest-arbitration language either -- also asymmetric in outcome, via a different textual mechanism than Ohio. Running tally across all currently-tested non-Ohio matched designs: 2 symmetric (Houston TX, Arlington MA), 2 asymmetric (Philadelphia PA cleanly, Trenton NJ via exclusion+external-deferral). This is now a genuinely mixed, unresolved pattern -- not evidence that Ohio is the outlier, and not evidence that Arlington/Houston are the outliers either. See state_city_claims_ledger.md National Claim 4 for the full synthesis.

hypothesis_text: Non-safety wage-setting more often routes through classification, wage grades, administrative pay structures, or narrower negotiated channels.
current_best_evidence: MA and OH non-safety salary schedules, reclassification, and grade language.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: Houston non-safety impasse evidence; some non-safety windows have not_found classification due windowing.
needed_state_types: matched-triad states with general municipal units
needed_source_types: non-safety CBAs; wage appendices; classification plans
next_sources_needed: Public works, clerical/admin, library, teacher, sanitation contracts in new states
decision_rule_for_strengthening: New non-safety units repeatedly show classification/admin mechanisms in matched settings.
decision_rule_for_weakening: New non-safety units show safety-like impasse/premium/minimum-staffing bundles at similar frequency.
notes: Non-safety is the bottleneck. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Reading PA library CBA and Trenton NJ AFSCME 2281 CBA are the two strongest uningested non-safety classification/admin-channel candidates found.

hypothesis_text: Staffing, continuous coverage, overtime, callback, and premium-pay mechanisms appear more institutionally central for police/fire than for many ordinary non-safety units.
current_best_evidence: Verified safety overtime, callback, premium, and minimum-staffing rows.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: Some non-safety rows have overtime/premium/classification; current codify is binary.
needed_state_types: matched triads including ordinary municipal non-safety units
needed_source_types: CBAs with work schedules and overtime articles
next_sources_needed: Police/fire and matched public works/clerical/library windows in new states
decision_rule_for_strengthening: Safety rows repeatedly show continuous-coverage mechanisms not mirrored in ordinary non-safety rows.
decision_rule_for_weakening: Non-safety rows in new states show equally central minimum-staffing/overtime structures.
notes: Need comparable windows, not just safety sections. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Paterson NJ's live TFOA staffing-level grievance (PERC-documented) is a specific, named candidate lead for this hypothesis, though not yet a document.

hypothesis_text: Peer/comparator wage evidence may be strongest in arbitration/factfinding/impasse contexts, but current evidence may under-detect it because of source windows/model false negatives.
current_best_evidence: One verified Somerville comparator row and documented San Antonio false negative.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: Single verified row; false negative; base CBA windows likely under-detect.
needed_state_types: states with awards, factfinding, wage panels, or impasse reports
needed_source_types: arbitration awards; factfinding reports; wage studies; comparator attachments
decision_rule_for_strengthening: Multiple awards/factfinding sources show grounded peer wage comparisons.
decision_rule_for_weakening: Comparator language remains rare even in awards/factfinding after verified full-window review.
notes: Highest-value source-type shift. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: no comparator-specific award/factfinding source confirmed yet; NJ PERC's interest-arbitration-awards index remains the most promising unexploited lead.

hypothesis_text: Texas-style unevenness may represent a broader pattern in states with specialized safety channels and weaker non-safety bargaining pathways, but this requires more states.
current_best_evidence: Texas has Houston matched, Austin safety-adjacent, and San Antonio safety-only.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: May be Texas-specific or source-production artifact.
needed_state_types: states with specialized safety bargaining and uncertain non-safety pathways
needed_source_types: state/city source scans; safety CBAs; non-safety availability records
next_sources_needed: Florida, North Carolina, Tennessee, Wisconsin, and selected Western states after tier-1 scan
decision_rule_for_strengthening: Multiple states show easy safety sources but weak or absent non-safety pathways under public search.
decision_rule_for_weakening: Public matched non-safety sources are readily findable in those states.
notes: Track source absence carefully, not as substantive absence. 2026-07-14: San Antonio sourcing pass found that TX civilian/general-municipal employees there are represented by a non-binding advisory committee (EMC), not a CBA, with wage-setting instead running through council-adopted Municipal Civil Service Rules -- consistent with Ch. 617's general bargaining prohibition and San Antonio not having extended a Ch. 174-style statutory channel to civilians. Read as a specific, evidenced case of 'weaker non-safety bargaining pathway,' not proof the pattern generalizes beyond San Antonio; support_level left at weak pending other-state evidence.

hypothesis_text: Ohio-style matched triads provide a cleaner design for comparing safety and non-safety bargaining under a shared public-sector labor-law framework.
current_best_evidence: Four Ohio matched triads under current evidence.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: Ohio may be unusually source-friendly; not national by itself.
key_attributes: all 19 attributes with matched triad design emphasis
needed_state_types: states with public police/fire/non-safety CBAs under shared framework
decision_rule_for_strengthening: At least two additional states produce multiple clean triads with codifiable text.
decision_rule_for_weakening: New states fail to provide matched non-safety sources or show incomparable legal frameworks.
notes: Design hypothesis more than mechanism hypothesis. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Philadelphia (PA) and Jersey City/Newark/Trenton (NJ) are candidate second-state tests of matched-triad generalizability, pending ingestion and current-cycle document confirmation (Jersey City's found documents are dated ~2009-2015). | 2026-07-14: Arlington MA (fire + public_works, exact-cycle 2021-2024) is the first non-Ohio matched pair actually codified. The DESIGN generalized cleanly (a real matched pair was findable and codifiable outside Ohio), but the Ohio triads' specific asymmetric-mechanism PATTERN (safety-only interest-arbitration) did NOT recur -- Arlington's non-safety row shares its safety counterpart's impasse-backstop and no-strike coverage. Read this as confirming matched-design generalizability while complicating the substantive-pattern generalizability; support_level for the design claim itself stays strong, but Philadelphia/Trenton (different state, different statutory framework) are needed before drawing a firmer conclusion either way. | 2026-07-14: Philadelphia and Trenton both codified. Philadelphia's matched-triad DESIGN worked cleanly (Act 111 awards for both safety legs, a real non-safety comparator for each). Trenton's matched-TRIAD design also worked (all three legs codified, genuinely overlapping). Both support H7's core design-generalizability claim (matched designs ARE achievable outside Ohio) even though their SUBSTANTIVE pattern on the impasse-symmetry question is mixed (see H2 notes) -- design generalizability and substantive-pattern generalizability remain two separate questions, exactly as flagged after the Arlington finding.

hypothesis_text: Massachusetts-style dense cross-occupation local comparisons are useful for mechanism development but may not generalize without more institutional contrast states.
current_best_evidence: Franklin and Seekonk supply dense multi-occupation comparisons; MA is not enough for national scope.
current_counterevidence_or_limits: MA has unique institutions and 16 contracts still uncodified.
needed_state_types: states with multiple occupations per city/town
needed_source_types: CBAs across police, fire, public works, clerical, library, teacher, other
next_sources_needed: Small/mid-city targets in tier-1 and tier-2 states
decision_rule_for_strengthening: Dense cross-occupation patterns recur outside MA.
decision_rule_for_weakening: Dense MA patterns fail to appear in other institutional settings.
notes: Use MA for mechanism development, not national generalization alone. | 2026-07-14: Arlington adds genuine within-MA institutional heterogeneity -- Franklin/Seekonk's codified rows showed NO interest-arbitration evidence in either safety or non-safety windows, while Arlington shows it in BOTH. This is itself evidence for this hypothesis's core point (MA institutions are not one uniform pattern) even before looking outside Massachusetts.
